claims.claim: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems must decide when to answer and when to abstain, yet the boundary conditions under which small instruction-tuned models fail to abstain remain underspecified.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-RAG Answer-Abstention Boundary Benchmark: A Pilot Study of Answer-vs-Abstain Failures on Real Documents with a Local Instruction Model

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We report a 32-example pilot benchmark constructed from SQuAD v2 development-set documents, covering four boundary types: plain supported questions, missing-evidence (unanswerable) questions, wrong-document retrieval distractors, and long-context needle-in-a-haystack cases.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-RAG Answer-Abstention Boundary Benchmark: A Pilot Study of Answer-vs-Abstain Failures on Real Documents with a Local Instruction Model

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Using a locally cached HuggingFaceTB/SmolLM2-1.7B-Instruct model on CUDA with direct Transformers inference, we observe that plain supported accuracy (0.625) substantially exceeds boundary accuracy (0.250).

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-RAG Answer-Abstention Boundary Benchmark: A Pilot Study of Answer-vs-Abstain Failures on Real Documents with a Local Instruction Model

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Missing-evidence and wrong-document conditions each produced 6/8 false non-abstaining answers; long-context needles produced 3/8 over-abstentions and 3/8 wrong answers.

claims.claim: These results suggest that the answer-abstention boundary failures previously observed in synthetic settings persist with real Wikipedia-derived contexts and a live local model, though the evidence remains limited to a single small model and a 32-example pilot.
